title: Adjoint Selmer groups for polarized automorphic Galois representations
speaker: Patrick Allen
abstract: Given the $p$-adic Galois representation associated to a regular algebraic polarized cuspidal automorphic representation, one naturally obtains a pure weight zero representation called its adjoint representation. Because it has weight zero, a conjecture of Bloch and Kato says that the only de Rham extension of the trivial representation by this adjoint representation is the split extension. We will discuss a proof of this case of their conjecture, under an assumption on the residual representation.

proof_stages.label: Geometric motivation and weight-splitting philosophy
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from étale cohomology of smooth projective varieties, recalls geometricity, purity, and expected semisimplicity, and motivates the principle that a non-split geometric extension of the trivial representation by a pure representation should force the subrepresentation to have negative weight.

proof_stages.label: Bloch-Kato Selmer formulation and adjoint target
proof_stages.summary: After fixing Galois notation and the coefficient field, the speaker interprets H^1 as extensions, defines the geometric Bloch-Kato Selmer subgroup as the de Rham/geometric extensions, states the weight-zero Bloch-Kato vanishing conjecture, and identifies adjoint representations Ad rho as geometric pure weight zero objects to which the conjecture applies.

proof_stages.label: Known adjoint-vanishing results and the reduced-R=T obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The speaker surveys prior automorphic cases, especially modular-form results using R=T theorems, and explains that modern higher-dimensional modularity lifting often gives only R^red=T, which loses tangent-space information and therefore does not directly imply vanishing of H^1_g.

proof_stages.label: Polarized potential automorphic main theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is stated for odd p and polarized Galois representations over CM fields that become regular algebraic polarizable cuspidal automorphic with adequate residual image after a finite CM extension, concluding that H^1_g(G_{F^+,S}, Ad rho)=0; remarks cover the totally real analogue, absence of level assumptions, independent work, restriction-corestriction for potential automorphy, and applications.

proof_stages.label: Deformation-theoretic tangent-space reduction
proof_stages.summary: Assuming the representation is already automorphic after the harmless potential extension, the Clozel-Harris-Taylor deformation theory supplies a deformation ring R and automorphic module M such that the tangent space of R at the automorphic point is the desired H^1_g, so it suffices to prove that the completed local action of R on M is faithful, equivalently that the completed local ring is just the coefficient field.

proof_stages.label: Patched-module freeness localized at the automorphic point
proof_stages.summary: Taylor-Wiles-Kisin patching gives a diagram with a patched deformation ring R_infty and module M_infty descending to R and M by the same equations; rather than needing global regularity of the local deformation tensor, the argument localizes and completes at the chosen characteristic-zero point and reduces faithful action to freeness of M_infty over R_infty there, which follows from regularity of the localized local deformation rings.

proof_stages.label: Local regularity criterion via obstruction dimensions
proof_stages.summary: For every place in S, known dimension formulas for local deformation rings and tangent spaces, using Gee and Euler-Poincare/Tate duality away from p and Kisin plus Bloch-Kato formulas at p, reduce regularity at the local point to the vanishing of morphisms from the local Weil-Deligne representation of rho to its Tate twist.

proof_stages.label: Genericity and local-global compatibility finish
proof_stages.summary: Local-global compatibility converts the needed Weil-Deligne vanishing into the assertion that the local automorphic representation pi_v admits no map to its Tate twist, and unpacking the local Langlands correspondence shows this is equivalent to genericity, which holds at all finite places for cuspidal pi, completing the proof.

examples.name: Punctured elliptic curve extension
examples.note: An elliptic curve over Q with an infinite-order rational point is punctured at that point and infinity to produce a non-split geometric extension whose graded pieces have weights -1 and 0, motivating the negative-weight obstruction behind the Bloch-Kato vanishing philosophy.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker constructs the Gysin exact sequence for the punctured elliptic curve, identifies the quotient generated by P−∞, pulls it back to a subrepresentation W, and analyzes the resulting nonsplit extension and its weights.

passes.reason: An ordinary punctured elliptic-curve cohomology extension is used as a non-example showing nonprojective geometry can produce non-split mixed-weight geometric extensions.
An ordinary punctured elliptic-curve cohomology extension is used as a non-split geometric extension showing the smooth projective hypotheses/semisimplicity behavior can fail.
An ordinary non-split mixed Galois extension from an open elliptic curve is used as a non-example showing smooth-projective purity/semisimplicity need not persist for open varieties.

A punctured elliptic-curve cohomology extension is an ordinary mixed/open-curve construction, used as a non-split geometric extension showing the smooth-projective semisimplicity/purity picture fails for open varieties.
A punctured elliptic curve yields a non-split mixed-weight geometric extension, used as a non-example showing the smooth projective hypotheses/nice semisimplicity-purity behavior can fail.
Used as a non-example showing open elliptic curves can give non-split geometric extensions, while the object is otherwise a standard mixed Galois extension.

It is used as a non-example showing that punctured/open curves can produce non-split mixed-weight geometric extensions, while the construction itself is not structurally extremal, degenerate, symmetric, or exceptional.
Used as a non-example showing that an open punctured elliptic curve can yield a non-split geometric mixed-weight extension, unlike the smooth projective semisimple picture.
used as a non-example showing an open punctured elliptic curve can give a geometric non-split mixed-weight extension
It is used as a non-example showing that an open punctured elliptic curve can give a non-split geometric mixed-weight extension.

used as a non-projective/open-curve case producing a non-split geometric extension, so it obstructs extending the smooth projective semisimplicity/purity picture, while the punctured elliptic-curve extension is not structurally sporadic, extremal, maximally symmetric, or degenerate.
Used as a non-example showing smooth projective purity/semisimplicity need not extend to open varieties via a non-split geometric extension from a punctured elliptic curve.

examples.name: Symmetric powers of a modular newform
examples.note: Potential automorphy of Sym^n of a modular newform, together with an embedding into an appropriate adjoint/Lie algebra representation, illustrates how the theorem gives vanishing for the weight-zero representation Sym^{2n}(rho_f) tensor det(rho_f)^{-n}.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the main theorem to symmetric powers of a modular newform under explicit hypotheses, forms Sym^{2n}(rho_f)⊗det(rho_f)^{-n}, explains its weight-zero status and embedding/direct-summand argument, and discusses the odd-n symplectic/orthogonal issue.

passes.reason: A standard infinite family of symmetric-power Galois representations of modular newforms is used as an application of the vanishing theorem, not to refute a claim.
Symmetric powers of a modular newform form a systematic automorphic/Galois-representation family used as an application of the theorem, not to refute a claim.
These are systematic symmetric-power Galois representations in an infinite family, used as an application yielding Selmer-vanishing rather than as a refutation.
Symmetric powers of a modular newform form a systematic infinite family of Galois representations and are used as an application illustrating Selmer-vanishing, not to refute a claim.
The symmetric-power Galois representations of a modular newform form a standard infinite family used as an application illustrating the theorem’s Selmer-vanishing consequence.
Used as an application of the vanishing theorem for a standard infinite family of symmetric-power Galois representations of modular forms.

It is used as a positive application of the theorem to a systematic infinite family of symmetric-power Galois representations, not as a refutation and not as a structurally special classification exception.
Used as an illustrative application of the theorem to a standard infinite family of Galois representations coming from symmetric powers of modular newforms.
used as an application of the vanishing theorem to a fully symmetrized tensor-power family of modular Galois representations
It is used as a positive application of the theorem to a systematic family of symmetric-power Galois representations.

used as a positive application of potential automorphy to prove Bloch–Kato Selmer vanishing for a systematic symmetric-power family, not as a failure case and not as a sporadic, boundary, collapsed, or maximally regular object.
Used as an ordinary application of the theorem to weight-zero twists of symmetric-power Galois representations of modular newforms, not to refute a claim.
